# Course Overview

`TEM 00UU Foundations of Cryptocurrency and Blockchain` is currently represented in
LearnForge as a draft course shell based on the current course sketch.

The course introduces how cryptography, data structures, networking, and consensus
make cryptocurrency and blockchain systems work. The teaching direction is
implementation-focused and assumes students can work with Python but do not already
know cryptography.

The current sketch has two unevenly developed parts. Part A is already coherent
enough to guide the first migration slices. Part B remains intentionally provisional
and will stay outside the canonical lecture plan until the scope is clearer.

## Knowledge Goals

- Explain the role of ledgers, hash functions, public-key cryptography, digital
  signatures, transactions, UTXOs, block structures, Merkle trees, consensus, and
  peer-to-peer networking in blockchain systems.
- Compare Proof of Work and Proof of Stake, including their security assumptions,
  incentives, and sustainability trade-offs.
- Describe the basic ideas behind smart contracts, Ethereum-style execution, and
  Layer 2 scaling approaches.
- Discuss privacy, regulation, business relevance, and market-facing questions around
  cryptocurrency and blockchain technology.

## Skills Goals

- Implement small Python exercises for hashing, signatures, transactions, block
  validation, proof-of-work mining, and peer-to-peer communication.
- Read and extend a simple Bitcoin-like code base for learning purposes.
- Translate technical blockchain mechanisms into clear conceptual and business-facing
  explanations.
- Scope and execute a substantial course project that combines code with conceptual
  analysis.

## General Competence

- Evaluate what parts of blockchain systems are fundamental infrastructure choices
  versus application-specific design decisions.
- Reflect critically on decentralization, security, privacy, regulation, and
  sustainability trade-offs.
- Communicate technical blockchain ideas clearly to audiences with mixed technical
  backgrounds.

## Planned Content Areas

### Part A: Cryptography and blockchain foundations

- Lecture 1: Ledgers, trust, and cryptographic hash functions
- Lecture 2: Public and private keys, digital signatures
- Lecture 3: Transactions and the UTXO model
- Lecture 4: Blocks, hash linking, and Merkle trees
- Lecture 5: Consensus mechanisms, especially Proof of Work and Proof of Stake
- Lecture 6: Peer-to-peer networking, gossip, and node discovery
- Lecture 7: Smart contracts, the EVM, and Layer 2 scaling
- Lecture 8: Ethics, regulation, and sustainability in blockchain systems
- Project framing remains part of the draft assessment description and is not yet a
  canonical lecture or assignment object in LearnForge.

### Part B: Business, markets, and forecasting

- The role of blockchain technology in business settings
- The market for cryptocurrencies
- Forecasting cryptocurrency values
- This part remains intentionally TBA in the current LearnForge checkpoint and is not
  yet represented by canonical lecture collections or reusable objects.

## Teaching Format

The draft teaching format combines lectures with guided Python exercises.

Part A is expected to stay close to the implementation-focused teaching style in the
current course sketch, with students building small components of a Bitcoin-like
system to understand the underlying mechanics.

## Assessment

The current sketch uses a semester-long group project rather than a traditional exam.

The project direction is still draft-level, but the intended deliverables are:

- a short mid-semester proposal
- final code and documentation
- a presentation or demo

The canonical LearnForge project brief should be authored later, once the assessment
scope, milestones, and evaluation criteria are stable enough to treat as source of
truth.
